title: Wooden Salt and Pepper Shakers
summary: A pair of turned-look salt and pepper shakers drilled from small hardwood blanks, sealed with a cork or silicone stopper.
description:
These shakers get their shape from a solid hardwood blank with cavities drilled into it, not from a lathe — a much more approachable first project if you don't have turning tools, and quick enough to knock out a set as a gift in under an hour.

The dispensing holes go through the top **in whatever pattern you choose** — more, smaller holes for salt, fewer, larger holes for pepper is a reasonable default, but there's no fixed spec here beyond drilling before you glue anything shut.

**Choose a silicone stopper over cork.** Cork looks more traditional but is genuinely harder to remove for refilling once it's been in place a while — a small material choice that saves real annoyance later.

tools.note: Clean, flat-bottomed cavities and stopper holes.

materials.name: Cork or silicone stoppers
materials.note: Silicone is easier to remove for refills than cork.

steps.title: Cut the blanks
steps.body: Cut two blanks 4" long from the 2x2 — a 2x2's actual size is 1 1/2" x 1 1/2", so each shaker is a 1 1/2" square post 4" tall. Square both ends carefully: the bottom has to sit flat on a table and the top is the face the dispensing holes go through. The 8 ft length yields far more blanks than the pair you need, so cut a couple of spares and keep the two with the tightest, most closed grain — open grain traps salt and is harder to keep clean. Mark one blank S and one P on the bottom before you start drilling, because they are identical from the outside once they are done.

steps.title: Drill the seasoning cavity
steps.body: Bore the main cavity into the BOTTOM face of each blank, not the top — the stopper goes in the bottom for refilling, so that is the end the cavity has to open out of, and drilling it from the top would leave no way to fill the shaker. Use a forstner bit for a clean flat-bottomed hole and stop well short of the top face, leaving a solid lid for the dispensing holes to pass through. Clamp the blank and drill against a flat surface so the hole runs parallel to the sides; a 1 1/2" post has very little wall to spare if the bit wanders. Decide the cavity diameter together with your stopper — see the stopper step, because that same opening is what the stopper has to seal.

steps.title: Drill the dispensing holes
steps.body: Drill the small dispensing holes down through the top face until they break into the cavity below — more and smaller for salt, fewer and larger for pepper is a reasonable default, and the pattern itself is yours to choose. Drill from the top face downward so any tear-out lands inside the cavity where nobody will ever see it. Drill one hole first and confirm it actually breaks through before you commit to the rest of the pattern. Do this before any finish goes on, then blow the dust out of the cavity.

steps.title: Drill the stopper hole
steps.body: Because the cavity opens out of the bottom, its mouth is the stopper hole: either bore the whole cavity with a bit matched to your stopper, or bore the cavity smaller and counterbore the bottom to the stopper's diameter. Size it against the actual stopper rather than a number — hold the stopper alongside the bit, then test the fit in a scrap offcut before you touch a finished blank. A forstner bit gives the straight, flat-walled hole a stopper needs to seal against. If you are still choosing, take silicone over cork: it looks a little less traditional but it still comes back out easily on the tenth refill, which cork does not.

steps.title: Sand, finish, and stopper
steps.body: Sand each shaker through progressively finer grits and ease the four long edges and the top corners so it sits comfortably in the hand. Apply the food-safe finish inside and out, including into the cavity, and let it cure fully before any seasoning goes near it — an uncured finish will taint salt. Push the stopper into the bottom of each to close it, and refill through that same hole from then on.
